content.text: A design review checklist for GenAI experiences serves a different purpose from a standard UX review checklist. Conventional UX reviews focus on visual consistency, information architecture, and interaction patterns. GenAI-specific reviews must additionally cover transparency (is the AI's role clear?), error handling (what happens when the model fails?), safety UX (are vulnerable users protected?), user control (can the user correct or override the AI?), latency UX (how does the interface behave during slow inference?), and scope boundary communication (does the interface correctly signal what the AI can and cannot do?).

attrs.alt: Design Review Checklist — Six Dimensions for GenAI Experiences

content.text: Users interact differently with AI-generated content than with human-authored content. Transparency requirements ensure that users know when they are interacting with an AI, that AI-generated content is labelled, and that the AI's confidence level is communicated in a way that enables appropriate trust calibration.
AI label on every AI-generated surface
Every response, suggestion, summary, or action taken by the AI carries a visible AI label — not just the first one in a session
If the behavior spec includes a confidence spec, the UI shows low-confidence markers (e.g., 'I'm not certain', disclaimer, reduced emphasis)
If CitationSpec.required is True, every factual claim links to a source — no invisible citations
The greeting turn communicates what data the AI has access to (e.g., 'I can see your recent orders')
At least one disclosure per session that AI output is not guaranteed correct — not buried in settings

GenAI features fail in ways that standard UI components do not: the model can return a response that is technically valid JSON but semantically wrong, take longer than the timeout allows, refuse a request the user considers reasonable, or produce output that a guardrail rejects. Every one of these failure states needs a designed UI response.
Loading indicator transitions to timeout message with retry option — UI never freezes
User sees a clear explanation of why the response cannot be shown, not a generic error
Refusal message is helpful and offers an alternative — not a dead end
UI renders differently (dimmed, disclaimer) for outputs below the confidence threshold
Slot fill failure after max retries
Fallback UI is designed (not a blank screen or spinner) — user is offered human escalation
Graceful degradation message with ETA or status link if possible

Safety UX covers the user-facing aspects of the feature's safety design. A technically complete safety implementation — refusal policy, guardrails, HITL triggers — can still produce a harmful user experience if the UI signals are wrong: a refusal that feels punishing, a consent screen that is designed to nudge users into data sharing, or a vulnerable-user path that is invisible.
Refusal messages use neutral language, explain the boundary, suggest alternatives — never imply the user did something wrong
No dark patterns in AI consent flows
Consent for data use, AI processing, or personalization is opt-in, unambiguous, and revocable — no pre-ticked boxes or forced consent
BLOCKING if vulnerable-user persona in PRD
Users in distress (detected by tone or explicit statement) see a human support path prominently, not buried
When the AI escalates to a human, the user is told explicitly — no silent handoff
UI does not use 'Act now', 'Only you', or authority framing to pressure users to accept AI recommendations

Users must retain meaningful control over AI-assisted actions. This is a regulatory requirement under the EU AI Act for high-risk features, and a trust requirement for all AI features. The design review checks that control affordances are visible, accessible, and actually work.
After any AI output, the user can correct, edit, or reject it — no locked-in AI decisions
Undo is available for irreversible actions
BLOCKING if irreversible actions in spec
If the AI takes an action on the user's behalf (send, book, delete), an undo window is shown
The user can request a human at any point — not only when the AI decides to escalate
At minimum a thumbs up/down; ideally a 'Why was this wrong?' flow that feeds the eval pipeline
User can start a fresh conversation without navigating away — especially important for long-form flows

LLM inference is slow compared to traditional API responses. A p95 latency of 3–8 seconds is normal for a single-turn response; multi-step agentic flows can take 30 seconds or more. The UI must be designed to handle the full latency range without leaving users uncertain about whether anything is happening.
Immediate response — no loading indicator needed
Skeleton screen or progress indicator with message
Stream tokens to screen as they arrive — do not wait for full response
Transition to timeout state with retry — never freeze

GenAI features that do not communicate their scope create false expectations that lead to frustration, trust collapse, and misuse. Users who do not know the AI cannot book flights will ask it to book flights; users who do not know the AI cannot access their account history will assume it can. The design must communicate scope at entry and reinforce it when an out-of-scope request is made.
Task scope stated at entry greeting
The first AI turn or UI copy states what the assistant can help with — not assumed to be obvious
When the AI refuses an out-of-scope request, it explains why and suggests what the user can do instead
No implicit capability promises in UI copy
Button labels, placeholder text, and marketing copy do not promise capabilities the AI cannot reliably deliver
If the AI's scope changes between sessions (e.g., new capability added), returning users are notified
Running the Review: Typed Data Model
The full review is a structured object, not a free-text document. A typed model allows the review result to be stored, queried, and referenced in the traceability matrix.

A design review with any BLOCKING failure cannot be signed off. The review must be re-run after the designer addresses all blocking items — advisory items can be deferred to the next design iteration.
Users do not know content is AI-generated; trust collapse when they find out
Add 'AI-generated' badge to every response card; include in design system component
Users think the page crashed; abandon and do not return
Implement loading states with progressive messaging: 'Thinking...' → 'Still working...' → timeout state
User is stuck; contacts support; support cannot help because the scope is product policy
Every refusal includes at minimum: what was refused, why (in plain language), what the user can do instead
AI sends an email the user did not intend; no recovery; user files a complaint
Implement a 5-second undo window for all AI-taken irreversible actions
User thinks they are still talking to an AI when a human has taken over; or vice versa
Explicit handoff message: 'You're now connected to a support agent'

GenAI interfaces introduce accessibility challenges that standard WCAG audits do not cover: dynamically generated content that may not trigger screen reader announcements, streaming text that updates faster than assistive technology can read, and AI-generated images or charts with missing alt text. The design review must include an accessibility sub-checklist specific to AI-rendered content.
Screen reader announced for new AI responses
aria-live='polite' on response container; does not interrupt ongoing read
Streaming output is debounced for AT
Screen readers announce streaming content every N tokens or on sentence boundary — not on every character
Every image output includes descriptive alt text, either AI-generated or from a template
aria-busy='true' on the response area during inference; aria-label changes to describe wait state
Colour is not the only confidence indicator
BLOCKING if confidence UI uses colour
Confidence level is communicated with text or icon in addition to colour — passes WCAG 1.4.1
Refusal messages do not rely solely on an icon or colour to communicate the boundary

# Accessibility annotation for the AI response component
# (pseudo-code for a React-style component spec)

AI_RESPONSE_COMPONENT_SPEC = """
Component: AIResponseCard

Accessibility requirements:
  - aria-live: "polite"         # announce new content without interrupting current read
  - aria-atomic: "false"        # announce only changed content (appended tokens)
  - aria-label: dynamic         # "{feature_name} response" during load; "{feature_name} result" when complete
  - role: "region"              # landmark for screen reader navigation

Confidence indicator:
  - If confidence == low:
      text: "Low confidence - verify this information"
      icon: warning triangle (aria-label="low confidence")
      colour: amber (supplementary only, not sole indicator)

Image output:
  - src: base64 or URL
  - alt: AI-generated description (from model) OR fallback template
  - If model cannot generate alt text: alt="AI-generated image, description unavailable"

Loading state:
  - aria-busy: "true" on response container
  - aria-label: "Generating response..."
  - visible text OR spinner with aria-label (not just CSS animation)

Streaming:
  - Batch token announcements: announce on sentence boundary (". " detected) OR every 2 seconds
  - Do not announce every character — causes screen reader flood
"""

GenAI features typically introduce new UI patterns that are not in the existing design system: confidence badges, AI attribution chips, streaming text containers, refusal message cards, and HITL handoff banners. Before design sign-off, these components must be documented in the design system and reviewed for consistency across the product.
Consistent label ('AI-generated', 'Powered by AI') across all surfaces
Same chip used in all GenAI features — not invented per feature
Standard set: HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW with icon + text + colour
Badge does not use colour as the only differentiator
Standard layout: icon + short reason + alternative action
Same card used for all refusals — not a raw error toast
Cursor animation during streaming; no layout shift on completion
Container height fixed or min-height set — text expansion does not push other content
Standard banner: avatar + 'You are now speaking with [name]'
Appears in same position across all flows — not custom per feature
Same widget across all AI surfaces — results feed same eval pipeline

If a GenAI feature introduces a new component that does not exist in the design system, the component must be reviewed and approved by the design system team before the feature can be signed off. Ad-hoc components create fragmentation that is expensive to unify later.

The design review must be run against each persona in the PRD, not just the primary user. Low-AI-literacy users, non-native language speakers, and vulnerable users may experience the same design very differently. The persona-specific review adds checks that apply only to certain personas.

In practice, the persona design check adds 3–8 checklist items per persona. A feature with four personas — urban professional, low-AI-literacy user, non-native speaker, and vulnerable user — will have 12–30 additional checks beyond the six standard dimensions.
Design Sign-Off Gate and Artefact Freeze

Design sign-off is the formal event that freezes the visual and interaction design. After sign-off, design changes follow the same scope-change protocol as prompt changes — they require documented approval and may invalidate earlier gate decisions.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class DesignSignOff:
    review_id: str
    flow_id: str
    signed_off_by: str       # designer who led the review
    co_signed_by: list[str]  # safety reviewer, accessibility specialist, etc.
    frozen_at: str           # ISO-8601 — set when all blocking issues resolved
    blocking_issues_resolved: int
    advisory_issues_deferred: int
    next_review_trigger: str # condition under which a re-review is needed

    @property
    def is_valid(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self.frozen_at) and self.blocking_issues_resolved >= 0

    def invalidating_changes(self) -> list[str]:
        """Changes after freeze that require design re-review."""
        return [
            "Refusal message text or tone changed",
            "AI labelling removed from any surface",
            "Confidence indicator threshold changed",
            "New AI capability added to the flow",
            "HITL handoff banner removed or repositioned",
            "Feedback widget removed",
        ]


# Example: how the sign-off gate connects to the handoff package
def can_engineering_begin(
    design_sign_off: Optional[DesignSignOff],
    prd_approval: bool,
    traceability_complete: bool,
) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    if design_sign_off is None or not design_sign_off.is_valid:
        return False, "Design sign-off not completed"
    if not prd_approval:
        return False, "PRD Gate 4 approval not obtained"
    if not traceability_complete:
        return False, "Traceability matrix has gaps"
    return True, "All prerequisites met — engineering may begin"

The GenAI design review checklist covers six dimensions that go beyond standard UX review: transparency (AI labelling, confidence disclosure, attribution), error handling (timeout, guardrail block, refusal, fallback), safety UX (non-punishing refusals, no dark patterns, distress path), user control (correction, undo, human override, feedback), latency UX (spinner at 500ms, skeleton at 2s, streaming at 3s, progress for agentic flows), and scope boundary communication (task scope at entry, informative out-of-scope responses). All BLOCKING items must resolve before design sign-off. The review result is a structured typed object stored in the handoff package and referenced in the traceability matrix.
: AI label on every surface, confidence communicated, source attribution if required
: every failure state designed — timeout, guardrail block, refusal, slot failure, API error
: non-punishing refusals, no dark patterns, distress path visible, HITL handoff explicit
: correction always possible, undo for irreversible actions, human override explicit, feedback mechanism
: spinner ≥500ms, skeleton ≥2s, streaming ≥3s, step progress for agentic flows, timeout state
: task scope at entry, informative out-of-scope responses, no implicit capability promises
